From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Nationalism that asserts that the English are a nation

Not to be confused with [British nationalism](/wiki/British_nationalism "British nationalism").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/be/Flag_of_England.svg/250px-Flag_of_England.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_England.svg)

[Flag of England](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Statue_d%27Alfred_le_Grand_%C3%A0_Winchester.jpg/250px-Statue_d%27Alfred_le_Grand_%C3%A0_Winchester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_d%27Alfred_le_Grand_%C3%A0_Winchester.jpg)

Statue of [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great"), the Anglo-Saxon King of Wessex from 871 to 899.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Map_of_England_within_the_United_Kingdom.svg/250px-Map_of_England_within_the_United_Kingdom.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_England_within_the_United_Kingdom.svg)

A map of [England](/wiki/England "England") (dark red) within the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") (light red)

**English nationalism** is a nationalism that asserts that the English are a [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") and promotes the cultural unity of [English people](/wiki/English_people "English people"). In a general sense, it comprises political and social movements and sentiment inspired by a love for [English culture](/wiki/English_culture "English culture"), [language](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [history](/wiki/History_of_England "History of England"), and a sense of pride in [England](/wiki/England "England") and the English people. English nationalists often see themselves as predominantly English rather than [British](/wiki/British_people "British people").

At political level, some English nationalists – such as the [English Democrats](/wiki/English_Democrats "English Democrats") – have advocated [self-government](/wiki/Self-government "Self-government") for England. This could take the form of either a [devolved English parliament](/wiki/Devolved_English_parliament "Devolved English parliament") [within the United Kingdom](/wiki/West_Lothian_question "West Lothian question") or the re-establishment of an [independent](/wiki/English_independence "English independence") sovereign state of England outside the United Kingdom.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The history of English nationalism is a contested area of scholarship. The historian [Adrian Hastings](/wiki/Adrian_Hastings "Adrian Hastings") has written that: "One can find historians to date 'the dawn of English national consciousness' (or some such phrase) in almost every century from the eighth to the nineteenth".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

### Anglo-Saxon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Anglo-Saxon")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/The_Venerable_Bede_translates_John_1902.jpg/250px-The_Venerable_Bede_translates_John_1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Venerable_Bede_translates_John_1902.jpg)

The Venerable [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")

[Patrick Wormald](/wiki/Patrick_Wormald "Patrick Wormald") has claimed that England was a nation by the time of the [Venerable Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede"), who wrote the *[Historia ecclesiastica gentis Anglorum](/wiki/Historia_ecclesiastica_gentis_Anglorum "Historia ecclesiastica gentis Anglorum")* (*Ecclesiastical History of the English People*) around 730.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Wormald attributes Bede with a decisive "role in defining English national identity and English national destiny".[[3]](#cite_note-3) Bede uses the label "English" to describe the [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") who inhabited Britain: [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") and [Jutes](/wiki/Jutes "Jutes"), and excluding [Britons](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)"), [Scots](/wiki/Scoti "Scoti") and [Picts](/wiki/Picts "Picts").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the final paragraph to the preface of the *Ecclesiastical History of the English People* Bede departs from the usual word "*gens*" and instead uses the word "*[natio](/wiki/Nation "Nation")*" to describe the "*historia nostrae nationis*": the history of our own nation. This is the first verbal appearance of the [English nation](/wiki/English_nation "English nation").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The Anglo-Saxon poem *[The Battle of Maldon](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Maldon "The Battle of Maldon")* described the eponymous battle between the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") forces of [Ethelred the Unready](/wiki/Ethelred_the_Unready "Ethelred the Unready") against a [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") invasion in 991. The poem praises the Anglo-Saxons' defence of "their land, the land of Ethelred the King, the place and the people" and [Byrhtnoth](/wiki/Byrhtnoth "Byrhtnoth"), [Earl of Essex](/wiki/Earl_of_Essex "Earl of Essex"), is attributed as saying: "Shall our people, our nation, bear you to go hence with our gold?"[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Both Hastings and James Campbell believe England was a [nation-state](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state") during late Anglo-Saxon times. Campbell writes that by the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") of 1066, "England was by then a nation-state".[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Medieval

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Medieval")]

See also: [England in the Middle Ages](/wiki/England_in_the_Middle_Ages "England in the Middle Ages")

The [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") introduced a ruling class over England who displaced English land owners and clergy, and who spoke only [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language"); however, it is likely many (if not most) were conversant in English from the second generation onwards. [William of Malmesbury](/wiki/William_of_Malmesbury "William of Malmesbury"), a [chronicler](/wiki/Chronicler "Chronicler") of mixed [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Normans "Anglo-Normans") descent writing in the twelfth century, described the [Battle of Hastings](/wiki/Battle_of_Hastings "Battle of Hastings") as: "That fatal day for England, the sad destruction of our dear country [*dulcis patrie*]".[[8]](#cite_note-8) He also lamented: "England has become the habitation of outsiders and the dominion of foreigners. Today, no Englishman is [earl](/wiki/Earl "Earl"), [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop"), or [abbot](/wiki/Abbot "Abbot"), and newcomers gnaw away at the riches and very innards of England; nor is there any hope for an end of this misery".[[9]](#cite_note-9) Another chronicler, [Robert of Gloucester](/wiki/Robert_of_Gloucester_(historian) "Robert of Gloucester (historian)"), speaking in part of earlier centuries, in the mid to late thirteenth century:

> ...the Norman could not speak anything then except their own speech, and they spoke French as they had done at home, and had their children taught it, too, so that important men in this country who come from their stock all keep to that same speech that they derived from them; because, unless a man knows French, he is thought little of. But humble men keep to English and their own speech still. I reckon there are no countries in the whole world that do not keep to their own speech, except England only.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

King [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"), himself a [Norman-French](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language") speaker, when issuing writs for summoning Parliament in 1295, claimed that the [King of France](/wiki/Philip_IV_of_France "Philip IV of France") planned to invade England and extinguish [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English"), "a truly detestable plan which may God avert".[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the *[Cursor Mundi](/wiki/Cursor_Mundi "Cursor Mundi")*, an anonymous religious poem in northern Middle English dating from approximately 1300, appears the words: "Of Ingland the nacion".[[13]](#cite_note-Hastings,_p._15-13) The Prologue starts:

> :   Efter haly kyrces state
> :   Þis ilke bok it es translate,
> :   Into Inglis tong to rede,
> :   For þe love of Inglis lede,
> :   Inglis lede of Ingeland,
> :   For þe commun at understand.
> :   Frankis rimes here I redd
> :   Comunlik in ilk a sted;
> :   Mast es it wroght for Frankis man —
> :   Quat is for him na Frankis can?
> :   Of Ingeland þe nacioun,
> :   Es Inglis man þar in commun.
> :   Þe speche þat man with mast may spede,
> :   Mast þarwith to speke war nede.
> :   Selden was for ani chance
> :   Praised Inglis tong in France;
> :   Give we ilk an þar langage,
> :   Me think we do þam non outrage.
> :   To lauid Inglis man I spell...

This can be translated into modern English as:

> This same book is translated, in accordance with the dignity of Holy Church, into the English tongue to be read, for love of the English people, the English people of England, for the common people to understand. I have normally read French verses everywhere here; it is mostly done for the Frenchman – what is there for him who knows no French? As for the nation of England, it is an Englishman who is usually there. It ought to be necessary to speak mostly the speech that one can best get on with. Seldom has the English tongue by any chance been praised in France; if we give everyone their own language, it seems to me we are doing them no injury. I am speaking to the English layman...[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 1323, Henry Lambard, a cleric, was brought before a court and asked how he wished to clear himself of charges of theft. Lambard said in English that he was a cleric and was then asked if he knew Latin or French. He replied that he was English, and English-born, and that to speak in his mother tongue was proper. He refused to speak any other language except English. Refusing to give any other answer to the court, he was committed to another court to suffer *[peine forte et dure](/wiki/Peine_forte_et_dure "Peine forte et dure")*.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

During the later decades of the fourteenth century English started to come back into official use. The [Pleading in English Act 1362](/wiki/Pleading_in_English_Act_1362 "Pleading in English Act 1362") sought to replace French with English for all pleas in courts. The Mercers' Petition to Parliament of 1386 is the oldest piece of parliamentary English; the earliest English wills at the London Court of Probate date from 1387; the earliest English returns of the ordinances, usages, holdings of the gilds are from 1389 and come from London, [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") and [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn").[[16]](#cite_note-16) [John Trevisa](/wiki/John_Trevisa "John Trevisa"), writing in 1385, noted that: "...in all the grammar schools of England children are dropping French and construing and learning in English...Also gentlemen have now largely stopped teaching their children French".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") with France (1337–1453) aroused English nationalist feeling.[[18]](#cite_note-18) [May McKisack](/wiki/May_McKisack "May McKisack") has claimed that "The most lasting and significant consequences of the war should be sought, perhaps, in the sphere of national psychology...For the victories were the victories, not only of the king and of the aristocracy, but of the nation".[[19]](#cite_note-19) In 1346, was exhibited in Parliament for propaganda purposes a forged [ordinance](/wiki/Ordinance_of_Normandy "Ordinance of Normandy") (in which the French King would have called for the elimination of the English nation) while Parliament was summoned to vote supplies to the king, who was engaged in the [Siege of Calais](/wiki/Siege_of_Calais_(1346%E2%80%931347) "Siege of Calais (1346–1347)"). After the Siege of Calais of 1346, King [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") expelled the inhabitants of that city because, in his words, "I wolde repeople agayne the towne with pure Englysshmen".[[20]](#cite_note-20) When King [Henry V](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England") conquered [Harfleur](/wiki/Harfleur "Harfleur") in 1415, he ordered the inhabitants to leave and imported English immigrants to replace them.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/King_Edward_III_from_NPG.jpg/250px-King_Edward_III_from_NPG.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Edward_III_from_NPG.jpg)

[King Edward III](/wiki/King_Edward_III "King Edward III")

Edward III promoted [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") during his wars against Scotland and France. Under Edward I and Edward II, [pennons](/wiki/Pennon "Pennon") bearing the [Cross of Saint George](/wiki/St_George%27s_Cross "St George's Cross") were carried, along with those of [Saint Edmund the Martyr](/wiki/Edmund_the_Martyr "Edmund the Martyr") and Saint [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor"). However Edward III promoted St George over the previous national saints of St Edmund, St Edward the Confessor and [Saint Gregory the Great](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) On 13 August 1351 St George was celebrated as "the blessed George, the most invincible athlete of Christ, whose name and protection the English race invoke as that of their patron, in war especially".[[24]](#cite_note-Summerson-24) In [Chichester](/wiki/Chichester "Chichester") in 1368 a guild was founded "to the honour of the holy Trinity and of its glorious martyr George, protector and patron of England".[[24]](#cite_note-Summerson-24) The Cross of St George was used by Edward III as banners on his ships and carried by his armies. St George became the patron saint of England and his cross eventually became the flag of England.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[Laurence Minot](/wiki/Laurence_Minot_(poet) "Laurence Minot (poet)"), writing in the early fourteenth century, wrote patriotic poems celebrating Edward III's military victories against the Scots, French, Bohemians, Spaniards, Flemings and the Genoese.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

After the English victory at [Cressy](/wiki/Battle_of_Cr%C3%A9cy "Battle of Crécy") in 1346, a cleric wrote a Latin poem criticising the French and extolling the English:

> :   Francia, foeminea, pharisaea, vigoris idea
> :   Lynxea, viperea, vulpina, lupina, Medea...
> :   Anglia regna, mundi rosa, flos sine spina
> :   Mel sine sentina, vicisti bella marina.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In English, this is:

> :   France, womanish, pharisaic, embodiment of might
> :   Lynx-like, viperish, foxy, wolfish, a [Medea](/wiki/Medea "Medea")...
> :   Realm of England, rose of the world, flower without thorn,
> :   Honey without dregs; you have won the war at sea.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Shortly after Henry V's victory over the French at [Agincourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt") in 1415, [a song](/wiki/Agincourt_Carol "Agincourt Carol") was written to celebrate the victory. It started:

> :   Deo gratias Anglia redde pro victoria!
>
> :   Owre Kynge went forth to Normandy
> :   With grace and myght of chyvalry
> :   Ther God for hym wrought mervelusly;
> :   Wherefore Englonde may call and cry
>
> :   Deo gratias:
> :   Deo gratias Anglia redde pro victoria.

[John Wycliffe](/wiki/John_Wycliffe "John Wycliffe") (1320s–1384), the founder of the reformist [Lollard](/wiki/Lollardy "Lollardy") movement, argued against the power of the Pope over England: "Already a third and more of England is in the hands of the Pope. There cannot be two temporal sovereigns in one country; either Edward is king or Urban is king. We make our choice. We accept Edward of England and refuse Urban of Rome".[[30]](#cite_note-30) Wycliffe justified his translating the Bible into English: "The gospels of Crist written in Englische, to moost lernyng of our nacioun".[[13]](#cite_note-Hastings,_p._15-13)

The historian [Robert Colls](/wiki/Robert_Colls "Robert Colls") has argued that "by the middle of the fourteenth-century nearly all the requirements for an English national identity were in place", including a "distinctive sense of territory and ethnicity, an English church, a set of national fables, and a clear common language".[[31]](#cite_note-31) Scholar of nationalism [Anthony D. Smith](/wiki/Anthony_D._Smith "Anthony D. Smith") agrees to an extent, as from his [ethnosymbolist](/wiki/Ethnosymbolism "Ethnosymbolism") perspective the ethnic core necessary for the development of modern nations had begun to crystallise during the fourteenth-century. That would not be to claim however that 'an English nation had come into existence, only that some of the processes that help to form nations had become discernible'.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Tudor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tudor")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Enrique_VIII_de_Inglaterra%2C_por_Hans_Holbein_el_Joven.jpg/250px-Enrique_VIII_de_Inglaterra%2C_por_Hans_Holbein_el_Joven.jpg)](/wiki/File:Enrique_VIII_de_Inglaterra,_por_Hans_Holbein_el_Joven.jpg)

King [Henry VIII of England](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England")

The historian of the Tudor period, [Geoffrey Elton](/wiki/Geoffrey_Elton "Geoffrey Elton"), has asserted that the "Tudor revolution in government" under King [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") and his chief minister [Thomas Cromwell](/wiki/Thomas_Cromwell,_1st_Earl_of_Essex "Thomas Cromwell, 1st Earl of Essex") has as its chief ingredient a concept of "national sovereignty".[[33]](#cite_note-33) The [Act in Restraint of Appeals](/wiki/Act_in_Restraint_of_Appeals "Act in Restraint of Appeals") 1533 famous preamble summarised this theory:

> Where by divers sundry old authentic histories and chronicles it is manifestly declared and expressed that this realm of England is an empire...governed by one supreme head and king having the dignity and royal estate of the imperial crown of the same, unto whom a body politic, compact of all sorts and degrees of people divided in terms and by names of spiritualty and temporalty, be bounden and owe to bear next to God a natural and humble obedience.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

By declaring England to be an "empire", this meant that England was a state entirely independent of "the authority of any foreign potentates". Elton claimed that "We call this sort of thing a sovereign national state".[[35]](#cite_note-35) The Act outlawed appeals from courts within the realm to courts outside the realm. The [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") destroyed the jurisdiction of the Pope over England. England was now completely independent.[[36]](#cite_note-36) For this reason Sir [Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More") went to his death, because in his words: "This realm, being but one member and small part of the Church, might not make a particular law dischargeable with the general law of Christ's holy Catholic Church, no more than the City of London being but one poor member in respect of the whole realm, might make a law against an act of Parliament". He later said: "I am not bounden...to conform my conscience to the Council of one realm against the General Council of Christendom. For of the foresaid holy bishops I have...above one hundred; and for one Council or Parliament...I have all the Councils made these thousand years. And for this one kingdom, I have all other Christian realms".[[37]](#cite_note-37)

When [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England") (daughter of Henry and Catherine of Aragon) became Queen in 1553, she married [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") and sought to return England to Roman Catholicism. Elton has written that "In the place of the Tudor secular temper, cool political sense, and firm identification with England and the English, she put a passionate devotion to the catholic religion and to Rome, absence of political guile, and pride in being Spanish".[[38]](#cite_note-38) Mary wanted to marry a Spaniard and [Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor"), chose Philip II (also his son and heir). With this marriage, England would become a Habsburg dominion and it did for a short time (arranged marriages such as these in the sixteenth century had built up the Habsburg empire). England "played barely the part of a pawn" in the diplomatic battle between the great European powers (France opposed the match) and the marriage was widely unpopular in England, even with Mary's own supporters such as [Stephen Gardiner](/wiki/Stephen_Gardiner "Stephen Gardiner"), who opposed reducing England to "a Spanish colony".[[39]](#cite_note-39) Ian Archer has argued that "the possibility that England might become another Habsburg milch cow was very real".[[40]](#cite_note-40) A courtier, Sir [Thomas Wyatt](/wiki/Thomas_Wyatt_the_younger "Thomas Wyatt the younger"), headed [a rebellion](/wiki/Wyatt%27s_rebellion "Wyatt's rebellion") to try to stop the marriage, motivated by a "nationalist resentment at the proposed foreign king".[[41]](#cite_note-41) Supporters of the insurgency urged Londoners to join to stop the English becoming "slaves and vilaynes", which was met with the response that "we are Englishmen".[[42]](#cite_note-Archer-42) The uprising was defeated, and Wyatt at his trial justified his actions by saying: "Myne hole intent and styrre was agaynst the comyng in of strangers and Spanyerds and to abolyshe theym out of this realme".[[42]](#cite_note-Archer-42) Mary vigorously [persecuted Protestants](/wiki/Marian_Persecutions "Marian Persecutions"), recorded by [John Foxe](/wiki/John_Foxe "John Foxe") in his *[Book of Martyrs](/wiki/Book_of_Martyrs "Book of Martyrs")*, which were unprecedented in English history and resulted in an "undying hatred of the pope and of Roman Catholicism which became one of the most marked characteristics of the English for some 350 years".[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") (who succeeded Mary I in 1558) made a speech to Parliament on 5 November 1566, emphasising her Englishness:

> "Was I not born in this realm? Were my parents born in any foreign country? Is there any cause I should alienate myself from being careful over this country? Is not my kingdom here?"[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The excommunication of Elizabeth by [Pope Pius V](/wiki/Pope_Pius_V "Pope Pius V")'s papal bull (*[Regnans in Excelsis](/wiki/Regnans_in_Excelsis "Regnans in Excelsis")*) of 1570; the [St. Bartholomew's Day massacre](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Day_massacre "St. Bartholomew's Day massacre") of 1572; the publication of Foxe's *Book of Martyrs*; the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") of 1588; and the [Gunpowder Plot](/wiki/Gunpowder_Plot "Gunpowder Plot") of 1605 all contributed to an English nationalism which was "thoroughly militant and Protestant".[[45]](#cite_note-45) An example of this nationalism can be seen in Lord Chancellor Sir [Christopher Hatton](/wiki/Christopher_Hatton "Christopher Hatton")'s opening speech to Parliament in 1589 in the aftermath of the defeat of the Armada. It has been described as "an appeal designed to rouse both patriotic and ideological responses".[[46]](#cite_note-46) It was fiercely anti-Catholic (the Pope was a "wolfish bloodsucker"), execrated Englishmen who turned against their native country, and appealed for England's defence: "Shall we now suffer ourselves with all dishonour to be conquered? England hath been accounted hitherto the most renowned kingdom for valour and manhood in all Christendom, and shall we now lose our old reputation?".[[47]](#cite_note-47) In 1591 a John Phillips published *A Commemoration on the life and death of the right Honourable, Sir Christopher Hatton...*, which included the lines:

> :   You noble peeres, my native Countrimen,
> :   I need not shew to you my bloud nor birth ...
> :   Was not his hart bent for his Countries weale? ...
> :   Take courage then, maintaine your Countries right, ...
> :   To straungers Yoakes, your neckes doe never bow. ...
> :   Our gratious Queene, of curtesie the flowre,
> :   Faire Englands Gem: of lasting blisse and joye: ...

Sir [Walter Raleigh](/wiki/Walter_Raleigh "Walter Raleigh"), in his *A Discourse of War*, wrote that "if our King *Edward* III. had prospered in his *French* Wars, and peopled with *English* the Towns which he won, as he began at *[Calais](/wiki/Siege_of_Calais_(1346) "Siege of Calais (1346)")*, driving out the *French*; the Kings (as his Successors) holding the same Course, would by this Time have filled all France with our Nation, without any notable emptying of this Island".[[48]](#cite_note-48) Hastings has claimed that this usage of the word "nation" (used by [Dr. Johnson](/wiki/Samuel_Johnson "Samuel Johnson") in his *Dictionary*) is the same as the modern definition.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Strong support exists among historians and students of nations and nationalism for the idea that England became a nation in or no later than the Tudor period. [Liah Greenfeld](/wiki/Liah_Greenfeld "Liah Greenfeld") argues that England was "the first nation in the world".[[50]](#cite_note-50) Others, including [Patrick Collinson](/wiki/Patrick_Collinson "Patrick Collinson") and [Diana Muir Appelbaum](/wiki/Diana_Muir "Diana Muir") argue strongly for Tudor-era English nationhood.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Others including [Krishan Kumar](/wiki/Krishan_Kumar_(sociologist) "Krishan Kumar (sociologist)"), argue that nations arose only in the modern period and that England cannot be described as a nation until the late nineteenth century.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Stuart

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stuart")]

The idea of the [Norman yoke](/wiki/Norman_yoke "Norman yoke") became increasingly popular amongst English radicals in the seventeenth century. They believed that Anglo-Saxon England was a land of liberty but that this liberty was extinguished by the Norman conquest and the imposition of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism").[[55]](#cite_note-55)

[John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton"), writing in the 1640s, used nationalist rhetoric: "Lords and Commons of England, consider what nation it is whereoff ye are" and on another occasion: "Methinks I see in my mind a noble and puissant nation raising herself like a strong man after sleep".[[13]](#cite_note-Hastings,_p._15-13)

It has also been demonstrated by projects such as the Locating the Hidden Diaspora by Northumbria University that English communities in America and Canada had a clear sense of English ethnicity especially in the 19th century and set up many societies and organisations and celebrated English culture and traditions, such as the Sons of St George etc.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In her widely cited book, [Britons: Forging the Nation 1707-1837](/wiki/Britons:_Forging_the_Nation_1707-1837 "Britons: Forging the Nation 1707-1837"), [Linda Colley](/wiki/Linda_Colley "Linda Colley") argues for the formation of an English nation in the Stuart era.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## English nationhood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: English nationhood")]

There are few books which analyse English national identity. Liberal thinking has difficulty with English nationhood due to the association of Englishness with regressive and conservative values. Academics typically consider 'the English question' in a constitutional manner.[[58]](#cite_note-:0-58)

## Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modern")]

The English nationalist movement has its roots in a perception amongst many people in England that they are primarily or exclusively [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") rather than [British](/wiki/British_people "British people"), which mirrors the view in the other constituent countries. The perceived rise in English identity in recent years, as evidenced by the increased display of the [English flag](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England") (particularly during international sporting competitions i.e. [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") and [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship")), is sometimes attributed in the media to the increased devolution of political power to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland").[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The entanglement of nationhood and class in England means that nationalism is associated more with working class English people. England remains a place where 'hot nationalism' is avoided, which has an effect on expressions of national identity.[[58]](#cite_note-:0-58)

One possible incentive for supporting the establishment of self-governing English political institutions has been the [West Lothian question](/wiki/West_Lothian_question "West Lothian question"): the constitutional inconsistency whereby Scottish, Welsh and Northern Irish [MPs](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament") in the UK Parliament have been able to cast votes on bills which will apply only to England while English MPs have had fewer such rights in relation to Scottish, Welsh and Northern Irish legislation, which is in many cases handled by the devolved legislatures.[[60]](#cite_note-60) This anomaly was addressed in 2015 using the [English votes for English laws](/wiki/English_votes_for_English_laws "English votes for English laws") procedures to ensure that legislation affecting only England requires a majority vote of MPs representing English constituencies.

Many contemporary English nationalist movements are associated with support for right-of-centre economic and social policies,[[61]](#cite_note-61) but nationalists elsewhere in the UK tend towards a [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") political stance, as evidenced by the policies of the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party"), [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru"), and [SDLP](/wiki/SDLP "SDLP"). English nationalism is also often linked with [Euroscepticism](/wiki/Euroscepticism "Euroscepticism") and [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") has been described as a symptom of English nationalism.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Opinion polls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=English_nationalism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Opinion polls")]

A MORI opinion poll in 2006 commissioned by the [Campaign for an English Parliament](/wiki/Campaign_for_an_English_Parliament "Campaign for an English Parliament") indicated that support for the creation of an [English Parliament](/wiki/Devolved_English_Parliament "Devolved English Parliament") with the same powers as the existing Scottish Parliament had risen, with 41% of those questioned favouring such a move.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In the same month, an ICM Omnibus poll commissioned by the Progressive Partnership (a Scottish research organisation) showed that support for full English Independence had reached 31% of those questioned.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In November 2006, another ICM poll, commissioned by the *Sunday Telegraph*, showed that support for an English Parliament had reached 68% and support for full English Independence had reached 48% of those questioned.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

A study conducted for the Commission for Racial Equality (CRE) in 2005 found that, in England, the majority of ethnic minority participants born there identified primarily as being British, whereas white English participants identified as being English first and British second.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

A YouGov survey for the BBC in 2018 found young people are less likely to feel proud to be English than older generations; further, the further someone lives from London, the more likely they are to identify with a particular part of England.[[70]](#cite_note-70)
